title: Acute Myeloid Leukaemia
oneLiner: A clonal myeloid stem cell disorder resulting in accumulation of immature blasts in bone marrow/blood → life-threatening cytopenias, typically in adults >60.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Sepsis / febrile neutropenia** – high-grade fever (≥38.3 °C) with profound neutropenia (<0.5 ×10⁹/L). Death from septic shock hours after presentation.  
- **Leukostasis** – WBC >100 ×10⁹/L → sludging in cerebral/pulmonary microvessels → acute dyspnoea, confusion, focal neurology, visual changes.  
- **Tumour lysis syndrome (TLS)** – spontaneous or after treatment → dangerous hyperkalaemia, hyperuricaemia, hyperphosphataemia, hypocalcaemia → arrhythmia, acute kidney injury.  
- **Disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC)** – especially in acute promyelocytic leukaemia (APML, M3) → widespread bleeding, petechiae, oozing from venepuncture sites, prolonged PT/APTT, low fibrinogen.

Skew your initial “pattern”: **a pale, febrile patient with bruising and a very high or low WBC** – think AML first until proven otherwise.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms** (3–5 things you’ll actually see)  
  - Fatigue, pallor (anaemia)  
  - Fever ± rigors (infection)  
  - Easy bruising, epistaxis, gum bleeding (thrombocytopenia)  
  - Bone pain (sternal tenderness, long bones)  
  - Unexplained weight loss, night sweats (constitutional)  
- **Key exam findings**  
  - Pallor, petechiae, purpura  
  - Hepatosplenomegaly, lymphadenopathy (uncommon in adults)  
  - Gingival hypertrophy (especially M4/M5)  
  - Signs of DIC: oozing from IV sites, haemorrhagic bullae
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Aplastic anaemia** – pancytopenia but no blasts on film; bone marrow empty (not hypercellular).  
- **Myelodysplastic syndromes (MDS)** – cytopenias with <20% blasts; dysplasia in >10% of cells.  
- **Acute lymphoblastic leukaemia (ALL)** – blasts are lymphoid (MPO-negative, CD3+ or CD19+); often younger age, more lymphadenopathy.  
- **Myelofibrosis** – extreme splenomegaly, tear-drop cells, leucoerythroblastic film.  
- **Vitamin B12 / folate deficiency** – macrocytic anaemia with pancytopenia; try b12/folate levels before marrow.  
- **Viral infections (EBV, CMV, HIV)** – can cause cytopenias + atypical lymphocytes; serology helpful.  
- **Splitter:** *Blood film for blasts + cytochemistry (MPO) – MPO-positive blasts = AML; MPO-negative = consider ALL or MDS.*
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside (first hour)**  
  - Full blood count (FBC) + blood film – look for blasts, Auer rods (pathognomonic for AML)  
  - Coagulation screen – especially if bleeding (prolonged PT/APTT, low fibrinogen)  
  - Lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) – elevated in high tumour burden  
  - Uric acid – elevated → risk of TLS  
- **Bloods**  
  - U&E, creatinine, LFTs, calcium, phosphate, urate  
  - Blood cultures ×2 (febrile)  
  - Viral serology (CMV, EBV, HIV, hepatitis B/C) – for transfusion and risk  
  - Group and screen (G&S) – for transfusion  
- **Imaging**  
  - CXR – rule out infection, mediastinal mass (more common in ALL)  
  - Echocardiogram – if considering anthracycline therapy (check LVEF)  
- **Special tests**  
  - Bone marrow aspiration + trephine biopsy – diagnostic gold standard  
  - Flow cytometry – immunophenotyping (e.g., CD13+, CD33+, MPO+)  
  - Cytogenetics / FISH – t(8;21), inv(16), t(15;17) (APML), complex karyotype  
  - Molecular studies – FLT3-ITD, NPM1, CEBPA, KIT, IDH1/2  
- **Diagnostic criteria (WHO 2022)**  
  - ≥20% blasts in marrow or blood **OR** <20% with recurrent genetic abnormalities (t(8;21), inv(16), t(15;17))  
- **Never-miss test**  
  - **Blood film + bone marrow aspiration** – if you send FBC but ignore the film, you miss the blasts. Every pancytopenic febrile patient MUST have a film.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED)**  
  - If febrile neutropenia (temp >38°C, neutrophils <0.5) – start empiric IV antibiotics *per local policy* (usually piperacillin-tazobactam or a carbapenem)  
  - If WBC >100 ×10⁹/L with symptoms – **urgent referral to haematology for leukapheresis**; consider hydroxycarbamide ([check local dose policy])  
  - If DIC suspected (bleeding + low fibrinogen) – give cryoprecipitate + FFP; if APML suspected involve senior immediately  
- **Ward (first 24–72 h)**  
  - Confirm diagnosis (bone marrow)  
  - Start TLS prophylaxis – IV fluids 0.9% saline at 125 mL/h (if no fluid overload), allopurinol 300 mg PO/IV OD (or rasburicase if high risk)  
  - Transfuse platelets if <10 ×10⁹/L (or <20 ×10⁹/L if febrile, <50 ×10⁹/L if bleeding)  
  - Treat neutropenic fever with antibiotics × until afebrile/neutrophil recovery  
  - Central line insertion for chemotherapy (avoid femoral if thrombocytopenic)  
- **Long-term / outpatient**  
  - Definitive chemotherapy: induction (e.g., “3+7” – cytarabine 7 days + anthracycline 3 days) – start after bone marrow result, **follow local protocol**  
  - Consolidation chemotherapy or allogeneic stem cell transplant (based on risk stratification)  
  - Supportive care: G‑CSF (filgrastim) – consider per policy (not routine in all centres)  
- **Default Orders (if admitted)**  
  - IV fluids: 0.9% saline at 125 mL/h (2000 mL/24h) unless TLS/fever increases needs – adjust with daily weights  
  - VTE prophylaxis: **avoid** if platelets <50 ×10⁹/L or active bleeding; otherwise consider LMWH after discussion  
  - Diet: neutropenic diet (avoid raw vegetables, undercooked meat) – but evidence limited; most centres avoid uncooked fruit/veg  
  - Observations: 4‑hourly NEWS2; more frequent if febrile/bleeding
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line (induction)** – “3+7” (common UK regimen):  
  - Cytarabine 100 mg/m²/day continuous IV infusion for 7 days  
  - Daunorubicin 60 mg/m²/day IV bolus for 3 days  
  - ⚠️ **Doses are per local protocol – check your hospital’s policy**  
- **Common alternatives**  
  - Idarubicin 12 mg/m² IV days 1–3 (instead of daunorubicin)  
  - FLAG-Ida (fludarabine + cytarabine + G‑CSF + idarubicin) for relapsed/refractory  
  - ATRA (all‑trans retinoic acid) + arsenic trioxide for APML – **do not start without senior haematologist**  
- **Key Contraindications / Interactions**  
  - Anthracyclines (daunorubicin, idarubicin): cumulative cardiotoxicity – check LVEF; avoid if LVEF <50%  
  - Cytarabine: cerebellar toxicity (especially in renal impairment) – reduce dose if CrCl <60 mL/min  
  - Allopurinol: interacts with azathioprine, 6‑mercaptopurine – not usually relevant in AML  
- **Renal / hepatic adjustment**  
  - Cytarabine: dose reduce in moderate–severe renal impairment (CrCl <60) – [check local policy]  
  - Anthracyclines: reduce for high bilirubin (>50 µmol/L) – consult local protocol  
- **Most dangerous dose error**  
  - **Giving an anthracycline without a baseline echocardiogram** – acute cardiac injury can be fatal. Also, never give full dose in CKD/elderly without senior review.  
  - **Incorrect BSA calculation** – double-check height/weight; miscalculation of BSA by 0.1 m² can over- or under‑dose by 10 %.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute**  
  - Febrile neutropenia and septic shock  
  - Tumour lysis syndrome (especially day 1–3 of chemo)  
  - DIC (especially APML)  
  - Chemotherapy toxicities: mucositis, nausea/vomiting, cardiotoxicity, cerebellar syndrome (cytarabine)  
  - Bleeding (thrombocytopenia + coagulopathy)  
- **Chronic**  
  - Relapse (most common in adverse‑risk cytogenetics)  
  - Secondary myelodysplasia / treatment-related leukaemia  
  - Graft‑versus‑host disease (if transplanted)  
  - Infertility, endocrinopathies (after transplant)
sections.Prognosis:
- Overall survival: ~30–40% in elderly (>60 years) but >70% in younger, favourable‑risk patients.  
- Strongly driven by cytogenetics/molecular profile: t(8;21), inv(16) favourable; complex karyotype, FLT3‑ITD, TP53 adverse.  
- 5‑year survival <10% in adverse‑risk elderly.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always review the peripheral blood film yourself** – seeing Auer rods or blast cells can prompt immediate haematology consult before marrow results.  
- **Start allopurinol (or rasburicase) as soon as AML is suspected** – TLS prophylaxis can prevent fatal arrhythmias.  
- **In febrile neutropenia, take blood cultures and give first‑dose antibiotics within 1 hour** – every hour delay increases mortality.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not start definitive chemotherapy before bone marrow aspirate and full genetic results** – unless APML is strongly suspected (TLS/DIC profile with blasts and APML morphology).  
- **Do not transfuse platelets without checking for DIC** – in DIC, platelets are consumed rapidly; replace coagulation factors first.  
- **Do not ignore a high WBC in a patient who looks well** – leukostasis can develop suddenly; ask haematology to assess for emergency leukapheresis.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if** (rare in acute diagnosis, but for follow‑up of MDS or post‑consolidation):  
  - Fever ≥38°C or rigors  
  - New bruising, bleeding, or black stools  
  - Shortness of breath, confusion, or focal weakness  
- **Follow-up**  
  - Regular haematology clinic; interim FBCs as per protocol  
  - For patients in remission: long‑term monitoring for relapse and secondary cancers
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior haematology registrar immediately for:**  
  - Fever ≥38.3°C with neutropenia <0.5 (start antibiotics first)  
  - WBC >100 ×10⁹/L with any symptoms  
  - Suspected APML (bleeding, DIC, blasts with granules/Auer rods)  
  - Tumour lysis (K+ >6, uric acid >500, AKI)  
  - Significant bleeding (e.g., intracranial, gastrointestinal)  
- **ICU / Crash criteria**  
  - NEWS2 ≥7 or lactate >4 mmol/L  
  - GCS drop <13  
  - Hypoxia requiring FiO₂ >0.5  
  - Hypotension not responding to fluid bolus

rawMarkdown:
## Acute Myeloid Leukaemia
**One-liner:** A clonal myeloid stem cell disorder resulting in accumulation of immature blasts in bone marrow/blood → life-threatening cytopenias, typically in adults >60.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Sepsis / febrile neutropenia** – high-grade fever (≥38.3 °C) with profound neutropenia (<0.5 ×10⁹/L). Death from septic shock hours after presentation.  
- **Leukostasis** – WBC >100 ×10⁹/L → sludging in cerebral/pulmonary microvessels → acute dyspnoea, confusion, focal neurology, visual changes.  
- **Tumour lysis syndrome (TLS)** – spontaneous or after treatment → dangerous hyperkalaemia, hyperuricaemia, hyperphosphataemia, hypocalcaemia → arrhythmia, acute kidney injury.  
- **Disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC)** – especially in acute promyelocytic leukaemia (APML, M3) → widespread bleeding, petechiae, oozing from venepuncture sites, prolonged PT/APTT, low fibrinogen.

Skew your initial “pattern”: **a pale, febrile patient with bruising and a very high or low WBC** – think AML first until proven otherwise.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms** (3–5 things you’ll actually see)  
  - Fatigue, pallor (anaemia)  
  - Fever ± rigors (infection)  
  - Easy bruising, epistaxis, gum bleeding (thrombocytopenia)  
  - Bone pain (sternal tenderness, long bones)  
  - Unexplained weight loss, night sweats (constitutional)  
- **Key exam findings**  
  - Pallor, petechiae, purpura  
  - Hepatosplenomegaly, lymphadenopathy (uncommon in adults)  
  - Gingival hypertrophy (especially M4/M5)  
  - Signs of DIC: oozing from IV sites, haemorrhagic bullae  

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Aplastic anaemia** – pancytopenia but no blasts on film; bone marrow empty (not hypercellular).  
- **Myelodysplastic syndromes (MDS)** – cytopenias with <20% blasts; dysplasia in >10% of cells.  
- **Acute lymphoblastic leukaemia (ALL)** – blasts are lymphoid (MPO-negative, CD3+ or CD19+); often younger age, more lymphadenopathy.  
- **Myelofibrosis** – extreme splenomegaly, tear-drop cells, leucoerythroblastic film.  
- **Vitamin B12 / folate deficiency** – macrocytic anaemia with pancytopenia; try b12/folate levels before marrow.  
- **Viral infections (EBV, CMV, HIV)** – can cause cytopenias + atypical lymphocytes; serology helpful.  
- **Splitter:** *Blood film for blasts + cytochemistry (MPO) – MPO-positive blasts = AML; MPO-negative = consider ALL or MDS.*

### Investigations
- **Bedside (first hour)**  
  - Full blood count (FBC) + blood film – look for blasts, Auer rods (pathognomonic for AML)  
  - Coagulation screen – especially if bleeding (prolonged PT/APTT, low fibrinogen)  
  - Lactate dehydrogenase (LDH) – elevated in high tumour burden  
  - Uric acid – elevated → risk of TLS  
- **Bloods**  
  - U&E, creatinine, LFTs, calcium, phosphate, urate  
  - Blood cultures ×2 (febrile)  
  - Viral serology (CMV, EBV, HIV, hepatitis B/C) – for transfusion and risk  
  - Group and screen (G&S) – for transfusion  
- **Imaging**  
  - CXR – rule out infection, mediastinal mass (more common in ALL)  
  - Echocardiogram – if considering anthracycline therapy (check LVEF)  
- **Special tests**  
  - Bone marrow aspiration + trephine biopsy – diagnostic gold standard  
  - Flow cytometry – immunophenotyping (e.g., CD13+, CD33+, MPO+)  
  - Cytogenetics / FISH – t(8;21), inv(16), t(15;17) (APML), complex karyotype  
  - Molecular studies – FLT3-ITD, NPM1, CEBPA, KIT, IDH1/2  
- **Diagnostic criteria (WHO 2022)**  
  - ≥20% blasts in marrow or blood **OR** <20% with recurrent genetic abnormalities (t(8;21), inv(16), t(15;17))  
- **Never-miss test**  
  - **Blood film + bone marrow aspiration** – if you send FBC but ignore the film, you miss the blasts. Every pancytopenic febrile patient MUST have a film.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED)**  
  - If febrile neutropenia (temp >38°C, neutrophils <0.5) – start empiric IV antibiotics *per local policy* (usually piperacillin-tazobactam or a carbapenem)  
  - If WBC >100 ×10⁹/L with symptoms – **urgent referral to haematology for leukapheresis**; consider hydroxycarbamide ([check local dose policy])  
  - If DIC suspected (bleeding + low fibrinogen) – give cryoprecipitate + FFP; if APML suspected involve senior immediately  
- **Ward (first 24–72 h)**  
  - Confirm diagnosis (bone marrow)  
  - Start TLS prophylaxis – IV fluids 0.9% saline at 125 mL/h (if no fluid overload), allopurinol 300 mg PO/IV OD (or rasburicase if high risk)  
  - Transfuse platelets if <10 ×10⁹/L (or <20 ×10⁹/L if febrile, <50 ×10⁹/L if bleeding)  
  - Treat neutropenic fever with antibiotics × until afebrile/neutrophil recovery  
  - Central line insertion for chemotherapy (avoid femoral if thrombocytopenic)  
- **Long-term / outpatient**  
  - Definitive chemotherapy: induction (e.g., “3+7” – cytarabine 7 days + anthracycline 3 days) – start after bone marrow result, **follow local protocol**  
  - Consolidation chemotherapy or allogeneic stem cell transplant (based on risk stratification)  
  - Supportive care: G‑CSF (filgrastim) – consider per policy (not routine in all centres)  
- **Default Orders (if admitted)**  
  - IV fluids: 0.9% saline at 125 mL/h (2000 mL/24h) unless TLS/fever increases needs – adjust with daily weights  
  - VTE prophylaxis: **avoid** if platelets <50 ×10⁹/L or active bleeding; otherwise consider LMWH after discussion  
  - Diet: neutropenic diet (avoid raw vegetables, undercooked meat) – but evidence limited; most centres avoid uncooked fruit/veg  
  - Observations: 4‑hourly NEWS2; more frequent if febrile/bleeding

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line (induction)** – “3+7” (common UK regimen):  
  - Cytarabine 100 mg/m²/day continuous IV infusion for 7 days  
  - Daunorubicin 60 mg/m²/day IV bolus for 3 days  
  - ⚠️ **Doses are per local protocol – check your hospital’s policy**  
- **Common alternatives**  
  - Idarubicin 12 mg/m² IV days 1–3 (instead of daunorubicin)  
  - FLAG-Ida (fludarabine + cytarabine + G‑CSF + idarubicin) for relapsed/refractory  
  - ATRA (all‑trans retinoic acid) + arsenic trioxide for APML – **do not start without senior haematologist**  
- **Key Contraindications / Interactions**  
  - Anthracyclines (daunorubicin, idarubicin): cumulative cardiotoxicity – check LVEF; avoid if LVEF <50%  
  - Cytarabine: cerebellar toxicity (especially in renal impairment) – reduce dose if CrCl <60 mL/min  
  - Allopurinol: interacts with azathioprine, 6‑mercaptopurine – not usually relevant in AML  
- **Renal / hepatic adjustment**  
  - Cytarabine: dose reduce in moderate–severe renal impairment (CrCl <60) – [check local policy]  
  - Anthracyclines: reduce for high bilirubin (>50 µmol/L) – consult local protocol  
- **Most dangerous dose error**  
  - **Giving an anthracycline without a baseline echocardiogram** – acute cardiac injury can be fatal. Also, never give full dose in CKD/elderly without senior review.  
  - **Incorrect BSA calculation** – double-check height/weight; miscalculation of BSA by 0.1 m² can over- or under‑dose by 10 %.

### Complications
- **Acute**  
  - Febrile neutropenia and septic shock  
  - Tumour lysis syndrome (especially day 1–3 of chemo)  
  - DIC (especially APML)  
  - Chemotherapy toxicities: mucositis, nausea/vomiting, cardiotoxicity, cerebellar syndrome (cytarabine)  
  - Bleeding (thrombocytopenia + coagulopathy)  
- **Chronic**  
  - Relapse (most common in adverse‑risk cytogenetics)  
  - Secondary myelodysplasia / treatment-related leukaemia  
  - Graft‑versus‑host disease (if transplanted)  
  - Infertility, endocrinopathies (after transplant)

### Prognosis
- Overall survival: ~30–40% in elderly (>60 years) but >70% in younger, favourable‑risk patients.  
- Strongly driven by cytogenetics/molecular profile: t(8;21), inv(16) favourable; complex karyotype, FLT3‑ITD, TP53 adverse.  
- 5‑year survival <10% in adverse‑risk elderly.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always review the peripheral blood film yourself** – seeing Auer rods or blast cells can prompt immediate haematology consult before marrow results.  
- **Start allopurinol (or rasburicase) as soon as AML is suspected** – TLS prophylaxis can prevent fatal arrhythmias.  
- **In febrile neutropenia, take blood cultures and give first‑dose antibiotics within 1 hour** – every hour delay increases mortality.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Do not start definitive chemotherapy before bone marrow aspirate and full genetic results** – unless APML is strongly suspected (TLS/DIC profile with blasts and APML morphology).  
- **Do not transfuse platelets without checking for DIC** – in DIC, platelets are consumed rapidly; replace coagulation factors first.  
- **Do not ignore a high WBC in a patient who looks well** – leukostasis can develop suddenly; ask haematology to assess for emergency leukapheresis.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if** (rare in acute diagnosis, but for follow‑up of MDS or post‑consolidation):  
  - Fever ≥38°C or rigors  
  - New bruising, bleeding, or black stools  
  - Shortness of breath, confusion, or focal weakness  
- **Follow-up**  
  - Regular haematology clinic; interim FBCs as per protocol  
  - For patients in remission: long‑term monitoring for relapse and secondary cancers

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior haematology registrar immediately for:**  
  - Fever ≥38.3°C with neutropenia <0.5 (start antibiotics first)  
  - WBC >100 ×10⁹/L with any symptoms  
  - Suspected APML (bleeding, DIC, blasts with granules/Auer rods)  
  - Tumour lysis (K+ >6, uric acid >500, AKI)  
  - Significant bleeding (e.g., intracranial, gastrointestinal)  
- **ICU / Crash criteria**  
  - NEWS2 ≥7 or lactate >4 mmol/L  
  - GCS drop <13  
  - Hypoxia requiring FiO₂ >0.5  
  - Hypotension not responding to fluid bolus
